about.title: OtoReport
about.subtitle: Otoscopic reporting software
about.projectDescription: OtoReport is a software developed at TecMedHub for clinical otoscopic report generation. It arises from the need for a standardized and efficient tool to document otoscopic findings.
about.aiDescription: We are working on oIR, an artificial intelligence system for advanced detection and classification of otoscopic findings.
about.collabInvitation: Everyone is welcome to collaborate on this project.
about.findingsLibraryDescription: The OtoReport findings library is an open, collaborative repository that gathers standardized descriptions of otoscopic findings for clinical and educational use.
about.findingsLibraryContribute: We invite hearing health professionals to contribute new findings, corrections, or improvements. To collaborate, it is essential to follow the established ethical guidelines:
about.findingsLibraryEthics1: Descriptions must be based on clinical evidence and up-to-date scientific literature.
about.findingsLibraryEthics2: Do not include personal patient data or information that could identify them.
about.findingsLibraryEthics3: Contributed images must have informed consent and be anonymized.
about.findingsLibraryEthics4: Content must be respectful, objective, and oriented toward clinical-educational benefit.
about.findingsLibraryEthics5: Respect authorship and cite sources when applicable.
about.findingsLibraryContact: If you want to collaborate or have questions, reach out to us on Instagram:
about.equipmentDescription: Community-tested and recommended equipment for clinical use with OtoReport.
about.equipmentShareExperience: Do you use a video otoscope not listed here? Share your experience on Instagram:
about.labOrigin: OtoReport was born because TM Carlos Moreira from Hospital de Angol asked us for it. That's how TecMedHub works: anyone can bring us a need, and if we can, we turn it into a tool and share it with the whole community.
about.labLocation: We are a hidden lab in Building 500 at the Puerto Montt campus of Universidad Austral de Chile — a corner where medical technology and vocation mix with a lot of coffee.
about.labCoffee: If you ever pass by, the door is open and so is the coffee.

contribute.kofiDescription: OtoReport is a project with no external funding. We build it with passion, dedication, and the belief that open medical technology can improve hearing care. Every contribution, no matter how small, helps us grow and fills us with joy.
contribute.kofiCoffee: If it's a coffee... in any lab, coffee is the fuel of the heart and hands. Thank you for being here!
contribute.kofiButton: Buy us a coffee on Ko-fi
contribute.equipmentField2: A brief description of your clinical experience with it
contribute.equipmentField3: Approximate price or range (optional, helps the community)
contribute.equipmentField5: A photo or representative image of the device (optional)
contribute.equipmentField6: Your name or institution if you'd like to be credited

findingsLibrary.collaborate: Want to contribute clinical images? Select a finding or visit Settings.
findingsLibrary.editContributeDesc: You can contribute your annotations and improvements to the library. Upload your edited image or a new one from the contribution page.
findingsLibrary.uploadNew: Upload new image and edit it

contribute.title: Contribute image
contribute.success: Package generated successfully. Send it to TecMedHub for review.
contribute.instructions: A .zip file will be generated with the image, annotations and metadata. Send it to the TecMedHub team via Instagram or GitHub to include it in the library.
contribute.ethicsWatermark: Your name will appear as a watermark on the image, crediting you as the contributor.
contribute.ethicsLicense: The image will be published in the OtoReport findings library under an open license for educational and clinical purposes.
contribute.ethicsConsent: You must have the patient's informed consent or ensure the image does not allow identification of any person.
contribute.ethicsNoPersonalData: Do not include personal patient data in the image or annotations.
contribute.acceptTerms: I have read and accept the contribution terms. I confirm the image contains no identifiable personal data and I have the necessary authorization to share it.

education.title: Educational Material
education.subtitle: Interactive ear anatomy tools for clinical care support

modules.anatomyDesc: Explore the structures of the human ear in an interactive cross-section. Click on each structure to learn about its function.
modules.tympanumDesc: Visualize the tympanic membrane and its most common pathological findings interactively.
modules.eustachianDesc: Understand how the Eustachian tube works and how its dysfunction causes middle ear pathologies.
modules.animationsDesc: Interactive animations of ear pathologies and processes. Create and play educational animations with the built-in editor.

editor.title: Animation Editor
editor.noLayers: No layers. Add a layer to get started.
editor.name: Name
editor.selectLayerHint: Select a layer to view its properties
editor.selectLayerForTimeline: Select a layer to edit its timeline
editor.clipShapeHint: Drag the handles on the canvas to adjust the effect area
editor.selectImageHint: Select an image from the list

anatomy.pinnaDesc: External cartilaginous structure that collects sound waves and directs them toward the external auditory canal. Its shape helps locate the source of sound.
anatomy.earCanalDesc: A canal approximately 2.5 cm long that connects the pinna to the tympanic membrane. It produces cerumen for protection and has hairs that prevent foreign body entry.
anatomy.tympanicMembraneDesc: A thin, semi-transparent membrane that vibrates with sound waves. It separates the external ear from the middle ear and transmits vibrations to the ossicular chain.
anatomy.middleEarDesc: An air-filled cavity containing the ossicular chain. It connects to the nasopharynx through the Eustachian tube, which regulates pressure.
anatomy.ossiclesDesc: The three smallest bones in the body: malleus, incus, and stapes. They amplify sound vibrations approximately 20 times before transmitting them to the inner ear.
anatomy.eustachianTubeDesc: A duct connecting the middle ear to the nasopharynx. It equalizes pressure and drains secretions. It opens when swallowing or yawning.
anatomy.cochleaDesc: A spiral-shaped inner ear structure filled with fluid (perilymph and endolymph). It contains the organ of Corti with hair cells that transform mechanical vibrations into nerve impulses.
anatomy.semicircularCanalsDesc: Three canals in perpendicular planes that detect rotational head movements. They are fundamental for balance and spatial orientation.
anatomy.auditoryNerveDesc: The vestibulocochlear nerve that transmits auditory and vestibular information to the brain. It has two branches: cochlear (hearing) and vestibular (balance).
anatomy.clickHint: Click or hover over a structure in the diagram to see its description.

tympanum.normalDesc: Normal-appearing tympanic membrane: translucent, pearly gray color, with visible light cone in the antero-inferior quadrant. The malleus handle and umbo are visible.
tympanum.retractionDesc: The tympanic membrane retracts toward the middle ear due to negative pressure, usually from tubal dysfunction. The malleus handle appears more prominent and horizontalized.
tympanum.perforationDesc: A discontinuity in the tympanic membrane. It can be central or marginal, caused by otitis media, trauma, or ventilation tube placement.
tympanum.effusionDesc: Fluid present in the middle ear without signs of acute infection. The membrane may show amber color, air-fluid levels, or bubbles. Common in children.
tympanum.tympanosclerosisDesc: Calcium and hyaline collagen deposits on the tympanic membrane appearing as white plaques. Generally sequelae of previous otitis media and usually do not affect hearing.
tympanum.otitisMediaDesc: Acute infection of the middle ear. The tympanic membrane appears reddened, thickened, and bulging. Increased vascularization and severe pain may be present.

eustachian.infoDesc_idle: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the nasopharynx. Its main function is to equalize pressure on both sides of the tympanic membrane and drain middle ear secretions.
eustachian.infoDesc_swallowing: When swallowing or yawning, the tensor and levator veli palatini muscles open the tube, allowing air to pass into the middle ear. This equalizes pressure and maintains adequate ventilation.
eustachian.infoDesc_blocked: When the tube is blocked (by inflammation, adenoid hypertrophy, or allergies), pressure cannot be equalized. Negative pressure builds in the middle ear, causing tympanic membrane retraction.
eustachian.infoDesc_effusion: Prolonged tubal dysfunction prevents adequate drainage, causing serous or mucous fluid accumulation in the middle ear. This causes conductive hearing loss and a plugged ear sensation.
